steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says that neither History nor any works predating it mention the Round Table, yet Le Morte d'Arthur does. The blank must explain why Langley included something absent from History and earlier works.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — *Le Morte d'Arthur* is more historically accurate than *History* because the *Mabinogion* had not been written when Richard of Winchelsea was writing his work.: The passage never discusses historical accuracy, and the Mabinogion's timeline is irrelevant to why the Round Table appears. ✗
  • Choice B — when a version of an Arthurian legend contradicted the version in *History*, Langley preferred to include Richard of Winchelsea's version in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.: This claims Langley deferred to Richard — but Richard's History doesn't mention the Round Table at all, so deferring to Richard can't explain its presence. ✗
  • Choice C — Langley encountered the Round Table in a source that Richard of Winchelsea was not familiar with when writing his *History*.: If Langley used a source Richard didn't know, that directly explains why the Round Table appears in Le Morte d'Arthur but not in History or works that predate it. ✓
  • Choice D — Richard of Winchelsea's accounts of Arthurian legends in *History* are more similar overall in content to the accounts in the *Mabinogion* than they are to the accounts in *Le Morte d'Arthur*.: A similarity comparison between sources doesn't address why the Round Table is absent from earlier works but present in Langley's. ✗
C is correct.
